summary: Many websites accidentally create a Google search monopoly by blocking all web crawlers except Googlebot in their robots.txt files. Bell Labs exemplifies this by blocking archive.org and all search engines except Google and the outdated msnbot, making their content inaccessible to alternative search engines like Yandex and preventing historical archiving. This widespread practice, often unintentional, makes it nearly impossible for competing search engines to operate and has led to some crawlers simply ignoring robots.txt entirely.
key_points: Bell Labs and many other sites block all crawlers except Googlebot in robots.txt
This prevents both alternative search engines and Internet Archive from accessing content
The practice is often accidental rather than intentional policy
Competing search engines face barriers beyond technical challenges due to these blocks
Misuse of robots.txt has led crawlers to start ignoring it, making it effectively useless
money_quotes: Running a competing search engine and preventing a Google monopoly is hard enough without having sites ban non-Google bots.
I don't think that the common default many web devs have, of blocking everything but googlebot, is really intended to block competing search engines as well as archive.org.
This mis-use of robots.txt, where sites ban everything but googlebot, is slowly making robots.txt effectively useless, much like browsers identify themselves as every browser in user-agent strings.
Google has a turnaround time of minutes to hours on updates to this page, which I find pretty amazing... they must also be crawling millions or tens of millions of other sites on an hourly basis.
key_insight: Web developers are inadvertently reinforcing Google's search monopoly through careless robots.txt configurations that block all crawlers except Googlebot, a practice so widespread it's rendering the robots.txt standard itself obsolete.
